content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

MRI, CT, and angiography look like medicine. But underneath, they are also electricity, cables, cooling, service, and metals without which diagnostics simply do not work.
When we read news about new medical equipment, the first thought is normal and human: more patients will get access to diagnostics. A tumor may be found earlier. A stroke may be seen faster. Doctors may understand sooner what is happening inside the body.
But there is a second layer in these stories that almost nobody talks about.
Modern medicine has long stopped being only about a doctor and an examination room. It increasingly depends on machines that need stable electricity, the right room, cooling, service, spare parts, trained staff, and an entire supply chain behind the hospital wall.
WHO reported that, together with the World Bank and Iran’s Ministry of Health and Medical Education, it is helping roll out imaging services in public hospitals. The programme covers 65 public hospitals across 31 provinces. It includes 42 MRI machines, 15 CT scanners, and 14 angiography systems. The release also notes that, as of 31 October 2023, 27 MRI machines had already been delivered, along with the planned supply of 814 devices to 165 laboratories nationwide.
The numbers look like healthcare statistics. Physically, though, this is a very different story.
An MRI is not just a large white machine in a separate room. It is a magnet, cables, shielding, cooling, power supply, electronics, service access, and people who understand how to keep all of it running. A CT scanner is not just a “box for images” either. It has an x-ray tube, detector system, rotating gantry, high-voltage electronics, workstation, ventilation, and a need for stable electricity.
An angiography system is even more complex, because it is already a procedural room. Imaging connects with the table, monitors, injectors, shielding, sterile workflow, and dozens of small things without which the procedure does not move.
That is why this news is not only about 65 hospitals. It is about how healthcare access in 2026 increasingly depends on industrial access. You can buy the machine. But without a prepared room, reliable power, cooling, a service contract, or trained operators, access to diagnostics quickly becomes a nice phrase on paper.
WHO directly mentions the installation, training, and handover process. That detail matters. In the real world, medical equipment does not start working at the moment of delivery. It starts working when it has been installed, connected, tested, when staff have been trained, and when a system exists that will not fall apart after the first technical problem.
What interests me here is how one medical news item accidentally shows the same physical logic we see in AI data centers, robotics, defense electronics, and power grids. On the surface, these are different stories. Inside, many things overlap: power electronics, sensors, motors, cables, specialty metals, cooling, and the people who maintain them.
That is exactly why I started paying attention to NRED. Not only because of the copper narrative, but because of the broader critical minerals angle. Recent updates at Wilmac are not limited to the standard “copper-gold project” story. MetalCore identified a copper-gold-platinum opportunity, including Whipsaw Creek Placer in the Trojan-Condor block, where historical recorded gold production was about 3,460 grams, while platinum was noted as present but unquantified. On top of that, the POLARIS 16 occurrence is classified both as Porphyry Cu±Mo±Au and as Alaskan-type Pt±Os±Rh±Ir. For my watchlist, this is interesting because NovaRed is starting to look less like a one-layer copper story and more like a broader exploration setup tied to the metals modern infrastructure needs.
Medicine often sounds soft. Doctor, patient, diagnosis, care. But modern diagnostics rests on very hard things: metals, electricity, cables, circuit boards, cooling, service engineers, and logistics.
And that may be the most interesting part of this news.
When a hospital receives an MRI or CT scanner, it is not just receiving a new service. It is receiving a small technological node that has to be connected to a large physical world.
Without that world, even the best machine remains just an expensive object in a room.
